full_response:
We evaluate the expression \(\eta_{i\mu} \eta^{\mu i}\), where \(\eta_{\mu\nu}\) is the Minkowski metric with signature \((-1,1,1,1)\) and \(\eta^{\mu\nu}\) is its inverse. Both \(i\) and \(\mu\) are spacetime indices summed from 0 to 3.

Using the inverse property \(\eta_{i\mu} \eta^{\mu j} = \delta_i^{\ j}\), setting \(j = i\) and summing over \(i\) gives:
\[
\eta_{i\mu} \eta^{\mu i} = \delta_i^{\ i} = 4,
\]
since the trace of the Kronecker delta in 4-dimensional spacetime is 4.

Explicitly, in a frame where the metric is diagonal, \(\eta_{00} = -1\), \(\eta_{11} = \eta_{22} = \eta_{33} = 1\), and \(\eta^{00} = -1\), \(\eta^{11} = \eta^{22} = \eta^{33} = 1\). Only terms with \(i = \mu\) contribute, yielding:
\[
\sum_{i=0}^3 \eta_{ii} \eta^{ii} = (-1)(-1) + 1\cdot1 + 1\cdot1 + 1\cdot1 = 1+1+1+1 = 4.
\]

The expression is a full contraction of the (0,2) tensor \(\eta_{i\mu}\) and the (2,0) tensor \(\eta^{\mu i}\). Under Lorentz transformations, each tensor transforms covariantly, so their contraction is invariant. Moreover, the numerical value 4 is the same in all inertial frames. Thus, it is a Lorentz scalar.

\boxed{4 \text{ and it is a Lorentz scalar.}}

Confidence: 100%
